# MAIN ARTICLE


# DiZyme: Open-Access Expandable Resource for Quantitative Prediction of Nanozyme Catalytic Activity


Julia Razlivina, Nikita Serov, Olga Shapovalova, and Vladimir Vinogradov*

Enzymes suffer from high cost, complex purification, and low stability. Development of low-cost artificial enzymes of comparative or higher effectiveness is desired. Given its complexity, it is desired to presume their activities prior to experiments. While computational approaches demonstrate success in modeling nanozyme activities, they require assumptions about the system to be made. Machine learning (ML) is an alternative approach towards data-driven material property prediction achieving high performance even on multicomponent complex systems. Despite the growing demand for customized nanozymes, there is no open access nanozyme database. Here, a user-friendly expandable database of > 300existing inorganic nanozymes is developed by data collection from > 100articles. Data analysis is performed to reveal the features responsible for catalytic activities of nanozymes, and new descriptors are proposed for its ML-assisted prediction. A random forest regression (RFR) model for evaluation of nanozyme peroxidase activity is developed and optimized by correlation-based feature selection and hyperparameter tuning, achieving performance up to R2 = 0.796for K cat  and R2 = 0.627for Km. Experiment-confirmed unknown nanozyme activity prediction is also demonstrated. Moreover, the DiZyme expandable, open-access resource containing the database, predictive algorithm, and visualization tool is developed to boost novel nanozyme discovery worldwide (https://dizyme.net).


# 1. Introduction


Natural  enzymes  are  widely  used  in  various  areas,  such  as the  food  industry, [1] biosensing, [2] medicine, [3] and  others. [4-6] However, they are disadvantageous because of theirs low stability,  complex  and  expensive  enrichment  and  purification, and  the  establishment  of  large-scale  production.  Therefore, cheap and easy-to-produce  artificial  enzymes  are  increasingly developing.  Nanozymes  are  defined  as  'nanomaterials  with enzyme-like  characteristics', [7]   which  combine  nanoscale  and enzyme-like catalytic properties. [8] To  date,  the  most  common are nanozymes with peroxidase [9,10]  and oxidase activities. [11,12]

Other, more complex, for instance hydrolase, [13] catalase, [14] phosphatase, [15] laccase, [16] and superoxide dismutase [17] activities  are  much  less  presented.  Several  nanomaterials  have  multi-enzymatic activity. [18-20] The catalytic activity of nanomaterials  is  subject  to  MichaelisMenten  kinetics  and  is  standardly  evaluated  by  the  Michaelis-Menten  constant ( Km,  mM)  and  the  catalytic  rate  constant ( K cat ,  s -1 ).  Nanozymes  have  already  been exploited in cancer theranostics, [3,21,22] environmental protection [23] cytoprotection, [24] biosensing, [10,25,26]  and other applications, [27-29]  and of major attention is the ability  to  regulate  the  catalytic  activity  of nanomaterials by changing theirs composition, shape, size, crystal system, and surface chemistry. With MnFe2O4, [22]  Fe3O4 [30] it  was  shown  that  the  peroxidase  activity decreases with increasing nanozyme size, which  is  caused  by  the  decrease  in  surface-to-volume  atoms  ratio.  Masud  et  al. reported  a sevenfold  difference in the peroxidase activity of Fe2O3nanoparticles with  different  types  of  crystal  systems. [31] In addition to material characteristics, surface modification with polyethylene glycol, [32] dopamine, [33] and others molecules [34,35] affects nanozyme activity but modified nanozymes often show lower catalytic activity than 'naked' ones, while the stability and substrate specificity are greater. [36]  The development of new nanomaterials  requires  considering  many  parameters,  which  can be implemented and accelerated with computational methods. By combining the density functional theory (DFT) calculations of  thermochemical  energies,  the  most  energetically  advantageous mechanisms of the catalase, peroxidase, and superoxide dismutase activities of Fe3O4have been determined. [37,38]  Chen et al. [39]  demonstrated a higher CO oxidation reactivity of Au-Pd core-shell nanoparticles compared to Au nanoparticles, which was explained by the easier formation of peroxo-intermediates after binding the CO molecule to the surface peroxide groups using  DFT  calculations.  By  combining  DFT  calculations  of energy-based  descriptors  and  the  cluster  expansion  method in  a  multiscale  model  Teeriniemi  et  al., [40]   predicted  phase diagrams  for  rationalizing  the  composition  and  structure  of Fe/Ni-based nanomaterials for efficient CO methylation. Given the ever-growing complexity of nanozyme  systems, datadriven approaches start to infiltrate the field of nanozyme discovery  enabling  rational  hypothesis  formulation  and  in  silico

www.advancedsciencenews.com evaluation.  For  instance,  Li  et  al., [41]   produced  the  rational design of nanozymes where a hydrolytic metal-organic framework  nanozyme  was  created  using  a  data-driven  approach. Computational approaches such as DFT are effective in calculating and estimating energy descriptors of nanomaterials but remain complex and costly to apply to a large volume of chemical interactions. ML approaches, which are easy-to-implement for  the  evaluation  of  many  affecting  parameters,  have  been recently  developed.  ML  consists  of  trainable  statistical  and computational  models  designed  to  process  large  amounts  of data  to  find  patterns  among  input  data.  The  ML  algorithms have proven their capability for basic and applied research in materials  science  to  predict  properties  of  nanomaterials  for discovering  new ones. For example, MatCALO, [42]  based on a decision  tree  (DT)  algorithm,-basically  being  the  set  of  cascading  questions  leading  to  the  final  answer-is  capable  of modeling correlations between materials, processes and properties, and as result it generates hypotheses about material processing plans depending on the desired properties. Generative adversarial networks-comprising two competing algorithms, one  of  which  tries  to  generate  new  samples  based  on  the dataset and another tries to differentiate between the samples in a dataset and the generated ones-were shown to be able to propose new Mg-Mn-O composition stable crystal structures using a coordinate crystal representation based on the chemical  composition. [43] To  design  new  materials,  Ong  et  al. [44] have developed a deep neural network to predict the formation energy of garnets and perovskites, and an algorithm to identify ensemble spectra capable of determining media coordination and oxidation states with80% accuracy. Also, papers focusing on  predicting the catalytic activity  of  nanomaterials  have recently appeared, for example, ML algorithm based on single crystal atomic position parameters showed the ability to predict the  energies  of  catalytic  activity  of  Rh-Au  alloy  nanoparticles in decomposing NO, [45]  normally calculated by DFT. Fernandez et  al. [46] constructed  a  neural  network  that  predicted  the  catalytic activity and selectivity of Pt nanoparticles with an accuracy of0.9using  structural  descriptors.  Today,  ML  algorithms  are applied to the discovery and design of new materials, allowing scientists  to  perform  research  in  materials  science  more  efficiently  and  less  resource-intensive.  Recently,  open  resources with built-in databases and ML algorithms have appeared for predicting  various  properties  of  material;  such  resources  are extendable,  and  the  performance  of  the  algorithms  improves as the data accumulates. These resources allow to predict the pharmacokinetic  parameters  of  dendrimers, [47]   the  structure and function of proteins, [48,49]  the topology of the protein membrane, [50] etc. [51-53]

In  the  current  study,  we  manually  collected  and  curated most of  the  existing  metal,  bimetal,  metal  oxide,  and  bimetal oxide  related  nanozyme  data  from  the  articles  containing  all the nanomaterial characterization as well as measured catalytic activity, followed by nanozyme descriptors generation based on fundamental element constants, performed a meta-analysis of the data, tested ML models on nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity  with  hyperparameter  optimization,  and  performance evaluation (see Theoretical part in SI), resulting in predictions of enzyme-like nanomaterial activity with sufficient accuracy. In addition,  experiment-confirmed  unknown  nanozyme  activity

prediction was  demonstrated  with  sufficient accuracy  (see Experimental  part  in  SI).  Furthermore,  an  open-access,  userexpandable  web  resource  DiZyme  was  developed,  with  a  predictive model, interactive visualization tools, and the ability to re-learn on the updated data.


# 2. Results and Discussion


Current research ( Figure1 )  focuses  on  the  peroxidase  activity of  metal-,  bimetal-,  metal  oxide-,  and  bimetal  oxide-based nanozymes as a state-of-art, since these4classes are the most abundant among existing nanozymes, have unique mechanical, electronic, optical properties, and structure, easy-to-synthesize, and can be sufficiently expressed using numerical descriptors. Before building any predictive ML algorithm, good data mining should  be  implemented  comprising  data  collection,  missing data  treatment,  data  meta-analysis  giving  the  ability  to  determine  the  parameters  affecting  the  most  the  predicted  value, subsequent  feature  selection,  and  data  formatting.  Although artificial  enzymes are widely used in various fields of science, to  date,  there  is  no  single  extensible  open-access  database  of nanozymes.


# 2.1. Data Collection and Pre-Processing


To address the need for synthesis-by-design approach in nanozymes development, a comprehensive database (Figure S1, Supporting Information) of > 300metal-, bimetal-, metal-oxide, and nanozymes of total67individual compositions (Figure S2, Supporting Information) with peroxidase, oxidase and catalase activities,  consisting  of  its  fundamental  element  parameters as  well  as  physicochemical  characterization  of  nanomaterials was manually collected from > 100articles with valid data representation, material characterization, and measured acti vity, [18-20,22,30-35,54-145] as  well  as  the  databases  with  elemental properties (Table S1, Supporting Information). Catalytic activity expressed as Michaelis-Menten constant ( Km, mM) is the substrate  concentration  required  to  achieve  half  the  maximum enzyme rate, the catalytic rate constant ( K cat , s -1 ) is the number of  turns-number  of  substrate  molecules  that  each  nanozyme (which concentration is expressed in µ g mL -1 ) site converts into product per unit time, considering reaction rate ( Vmax , mM s -1 ) and  the  weight-to-volume  concentration  of  catalyst  ( µ g  mL -1 ). Missing data values were curated using the k-NN algorithm (see Theoretical part in SI), filling the missing values based on the closest samples, where the maximal data sparsity threshold was80%, motivated by the sufficient performance of the k-NN algorithm.  Fundamental  constants,  for  example,  electronegativity, electron affinity, oxidation state, ionic radius, and redox potential,  which  influence  electron  transport  occurring  during  the catalysis,  were used to characterize the non-oxygen nanozyme elements. Oxygen was only coded as the stoichiometric coefficient since it is the most abundant element in the nanozymes mainly comprised of oxides. The crystal system and zeta potential  reflecting the surface charge, stability, and surface adsorption  of  the  nanomaterial  involved  in  heterogeneous  catalysis, as  well  as  the  surface  area,  were  used  to  describe  material

physicochemical  properties  and  boundary  effects.  Enzymatic activity  assay  parameters  such  as  pH,  temperature,  substrate type,  nanozyme  concentration,  substrate  concentration,  and H2O2concentration were also included as strongly influencing the detected enzymatic activity.

Feature  selection  is  an  important  step  in  building  an  ML algorithm process since all the predictors must correlate with the feature and determine it to be predicted; moreover, intercorrelation  between  the  predictors  significantly  lowers  ML model  performance,  especially  when  using  a  small  dataset, which  is  a  typical  problem  in  materials  science.  In  addition, feature  selection  allows  to  reduce  the  number  of  predictors, which  further  increases  model  accuracy,  inter-,  extrapolative power,  and  prevents  overfitting,-which  is  the  phenomenon where the model fits exactly against the training data and fails to  extrapolate  on  new  samples-and  to  overcome  so-called curse  of  dimensionality,  originating  from  the  need  in  exponential  data  augmentation  with  the  increase  in  the  number of  dimensions. [146] For  better  functioning  of  the  model,  less inter-correlated  parameters  were  selected  from  the  collected database  using  the  correlation  matrix  algorithm  (Figure  S3, Supporting Information), where the threshold of Pearson's correlation coefficient-being the measure of linear association or statistical  relationship  between  two  variables-for  all  pairs  of parameters was0.5. The first  descriptor  was  the  electronegativity, which was recalculated to the average electronegativityconsidering  the  stoichiometry-of  the  non-oxygen  elements that make up the nanozyme due to the inter-correlation of the electronegativities of the elements (mX, Equation1). The mean charge density (mCD, Equation2) was chosen as a parameter to account for the charge distribution among the atom volume of  all  non-oxygen  elements  in  the  nanomaterial  composition.

It  was  calculated  as  the  average  ratio  of  the  oxidation  state and ionic radius (high-spin radii were used for3d-elements as most of the systems are oxides, where3d-elements exist in the high-spin state, while the low-spin ionic radii were used for the other  elements)  for  non-oxygen  elements.  The  average  redox potential  (mRedOx,  Equation3)  of  non-oxygen  elements  was introduced to reflect the reduction from a stable oxidation state to a zero state. Due to the strong correlation between the nanoparticle length, width, and depth, and extremely tedious evaluation of the real nanoparticle volume reflecting the nanozyme dimensionality,  a  single  'cell  volume'  parameter  correlating with  the  nanozyme  real  volume  was  introduced  (Volume, Equation4). Since mostly uncharged polymers and surfactants were  used  in  the  synthesis  of  the  collected  nanozymes,  categorical  parameters were chosen as the descriptors to account for  the  presence/absence  of  a  neutrally  charged  polymer  and surfactants  in  the  nanoparticle  synthesis  influencing  final nanozyme surface chemistry:

where  A x B y O z -chemical  formula  of  nanozyme; n -number of non-oxygen elements; X -Pauling electronegativity, eV;

www.advancedsciencenews.com mX-mean  electronegativity,  eV;  mCD-mean  charge  density; IR-ionic radius, pm; OS-oxidation state; RedOx-redox potential, V ; x , y , z -stoichiometric coefficients; A and B indicate that the characteristic belongs to the non-oxygen elements A and B, respectively.

Therefore, essential parameters to predict the catalytic activity of  nanozymes,  describing  the  elemental  composition,  material physicochemical properties, shape, size, as well as the synthesis  and  analysis  conditions,  were  calculated  and  selected  to describe the processes to be predicted comprehensively; moreover,  detailed  data  analysis  was  implemented  (see  Theoretical part  in  SI)  via  principal  component  analysis  or  principal  component analysis (PCA) (Figures S4and S5, Supporting Information),-enabling  high  dimensional  data  visualization  through the compression of the number of dimensions while preserving the maximal  differentiation  between  the  data  points-and meta-analysis  (Figure  S6,  Supporting  Information).  A  total  of227samples with an80/20split were used to train and test the model,  respectively.  The  processed  dataset  (227nanozymes) after  correlation-based  feature  selection  describes  i)  composition as mean electronegativity, mean redox potential, and mean charge density; ii) material properties as crystal system; shape as dimensionality; size as volume; iii) synthesis conditions as presence/absence of neutral polymer and surfactant; analysis conditions  as  pH,  temperature,  substrate  type,  H2O2,  substrate  and catalyst concentration (Table S2, Supporting Information).


# 2.2. ML-Assisted Prediction of Catalytic Activity


Parametric  models,  which  have  a  fixed  set  of  parameters  to tune,  such  as  linear  regression  (LR),  trying  to  find  the  linear dependence  of  the  predicted  value  Y  on  N  predictors  X i ( Y = β 0X0 + β 1X1 + … + β NXN + ε )  by  tuning the coefficients β i and ε ,  and  artificial  neural  network  (ANN),  attempting  to mimic the signal transduction in a human brain, as well as nonparametric gradient boosting regression (GBR),-using sequential  learning  instead  of  the  parallel  one  used,  for  instance,  in random  forest  models-support  vector  regression,  DT,  and random  forest regression (RFR)  models  (Figure S7,  Supporting Information) were fed14selected parameters (Table S2, Supporting  Information)  as  an  input, and  the peroxidase activity was represented as lgKm  and lgK cat  as outputs to provide the  model  ability  to  treat  equally  all  the  orders  of  magnitude such as between0.1/0.01and10/1. The model performance was evaluated  using  the  coefficient  of  determination  ( R2 ),-which is  a  statistical  measurement that examines how differences in one  variable  can  be  explained  by  the  difference  in  a  second variable-mean absolute error (MAE) ( Figure2A,B), leave-oneout cross-validation ( Q2 ),-where the model is trained to predict every single sample based on the other samples playing the role of  training  data-and  the  root  mean  square  error  (Figure  S8, Supporting Information) before and after the feature selection.

The6chosen ML models were tested at optimal parameters obtained with statistical Bayesian optimization (Table S3, Supporting  Information),  using  the  Bayes  theorem  to  tune  the model intrinsic parameters. Based on the analyzed metrics R2and MAE, the RFR model has shown the best performance and low  sensitivity  to  outliers  both  on  raw  and  processed  dataset

(Figure2A,B).  Moreover,  no  overfitting  is  observed,  which  is evident from Q2cross-validation metrics in the range of0.6-1 (Figure S8, Supporting Information). The lower performance of LR, expressed in low values of R2 (0.01for Km, 0.094for K cat  on raw and0.022for Km, 0.181for K cat   on processed dataset) and high  values  of  MAE  (0.977for Km,  1.865for K cat on  raw  and0.841for Km, 1.595for K cat  on processed dataset), can be due to non-linear relationships between the predictors and the catalytic activity  of  the  nanozymes.  ANN  failed  to  perform  a  sufficient accuracy  ( R2 0.129for Km,  0.276for K cat on  raw  and R2 0.472for Km,  0.656for K cat on  processed  dataset)  since  it  requires many  data  points  to  outperform  the  conventional  models.  It is important to note that DT, despite the similar learning principle, demonstrated twofold lower R2values (0.137for Km, 0.286for K cat on  raw  and0.473for Km,  0.660for K cat on  processed dataset) than RFR both for Km and K cat , which can be due to its higher sensitivity to outliers, considered in the RFR model by the collective DT prediction averaging. From the analysis of the RFR model performance, due to the feature selection and introduction of new descriptors, the prediction R2of  the  best  RFR model increased for Km from0.33to0.65 (Figure2C,E, respectively),  and K cat from0.52to0.8  (Figure2D,F,  respectively). MAE decreased from0.85to0.57for Km and K cat from0.98to0.78 (Figure2B). Therefore, the validity of the correlation-based feature selection approach is demonstrated as well as the best performance of RFR model in peroxidase activity prediction.

It  is  important  to  reveal  physical  insights  into  RFR  decision making (Figure S9, Supporting Information) usually realized  via  average  feature  impact  (AFI)  (see  Theoretical  part  in SI),  which  was  implemented  to  reveal  what  parameters  make the  greatest  contribution  to  the  model  performance  and  how exactly  they  determine  the  values  of Km  and K cat .  Besides  the analysis conditions, mX (AFI of0.50and0.23for Km and K cat , respectively)  turned  out  to  significantly  affect  the  model  performance (Figure S10A,B, Supporting Information), where the lower values of mX resulted in the smaller values of both Km and K cat   (Figure  S10C,D,  Supporting  Information),  which  can be due to the elevated electron transfer via less electronegative atoms. mCD (AFI of0.49and0.22for Km and K cat , respectively) was also one of the most important parameters influencing the model  performance,  although  no  direct  connection  between mCD and both Km and K cat  is observed based on the AFI analysis (Figure S10C,D, Supporting Information). Since K cat reflects the nanozyme specificity, it was greatly affected by the presence of  polymers  usually  serving  as  the  capping  agents,  shown  in the previous works to lower the catalytic activity on nanozymes (which is consistent with our data, Figure S10C,D, Supporting Information) while increasing its specificity; the opposite behavior was observed in the case of Km. Higher temperature during the analysis lowered the K cat  values while increasing Km values, which can be due to the increase in the number of effective collisions between the nanozyme, substrate, and H2O2 . Km value was also affected  by  the  surfactants,  where  lower  values were observed in the presence of surfactants, which can be due to  its  ability  to  result  in  smaller  nanoparticles  during the synthesis  and  alter  surface  charge, [147] the  opposite  behavior  was observed for K cat .

The samples outside the predictive intervals of0 | lg K | 1m ≤ ∆ ≤ and0 | lg K | 1cat ≤ ∆ ≤ , allowing  to  presume  activity  prior  to  the  experimental  data with  sufficient  accuracy  of1order  of  magnitude  to  differentiate  between  potentially  good  and  bad  nanozymes,  have such  under-represented  values  as  the  low  concentrations  of substrate, H2O2, and the catalyst, as well as the poorly represented types of crystal system, dimensionality, and substrate type.  Notably,  a  very  high  sparsity  of  some  physicochemical properties,  for  instance,  the  surface  area  and  the  involuntary  use  of  categorical  parameters  motivated  by  the  absence of  versatile  descriptors  further  limits  the  model  learning ability. Thus, there is an urgent need for proper system characterization in the articles and further meaningful descriptors development.

To test the model not only on unseen collected experimental data,  but  in  a  real  experiment,  we  have  also  implemented  an experimental evaluation on the ongoing nanozymes (non-transparent points on Figure4 , Supporting Information) synthesized and characterized (see Figures S12and S13and Table S4, Supporting Information) in our laboratory (See in the Experimental part of SI), which were uncoated oxides Fe3O4and ZnFe2O4, representing one of the most abundant nanozyme types (Figure S2, Supporting  Information),  as  well  as  amorphous  and  crystalline bimetal FeCo, representing one of the least abundant types (Figure S1, Supporting Information). Therefore,  the  model  was  able  to  presume  the  activities  of the  least  abundant  samples  despite  their  small  number  in the initial database, showing the model ability to extrapolate due  to  the  developed  elemental  descriptors. For instance, the best prediction was achieved on crystalline FeCo (sample14in Table S5, Supporting Information), where the predicted Km = 0.21and the real value Km = 0.24, and amorphous FeCo (sample10in Table S5, Supporting Information), where the predicted Km = 0.30and the real value Km = 0.39. The worse prediction was observed for ZnFe2O4  (sample7in Table S5, Supporting  Information),  where  predicted Km = 0.14and  the real  value Km = 1.38,  although  this  difference  is  still  smaller than  the  order  of  magnitude,  allowing  to  presume  its  catalytic  activity  prior  to  experiment  and  to  differentiate  between nanozymes  showing  good  or  bad  performance.  The  model ability to work on experimentally obtained data and to extrapolate on the under-represented samples was demonstrated, enabling  nanozyme  comparison  and  new  hypotheses  generation prior to experiment.


# 2.3. Open-Access Multifunctional Resource


Based  on  the  collected  database  and  the  selected  optimized model capable of quantitative predictions of peroxidase enzymatic  activity  represented  as  the  Michaelis-Menten  constant and turnover number, an open access web platform DiZyme (https://dizyme.net)  has  been  first  developed  to  provide  a publicly  available  resource  for  the  synthesis-by-design  development of novel nanozymes. The platform (Figure S14, Supporting Information) contains a built-in expandable database of  nanozymes  with  links  to  original  articles,  an  interactive clickable  tool  for  data  visualization,  and  ML  models  capable of  predicting  catalytic  activity  represented  as Km  constant with R2 = 0.63and K cat ,  s -1with R2 = 0.80for  various  levels of  user  requests.  However,  in  the  case  of  samples  whose parameters  deviate  strongly  from  the  parameters'  distributions of the training set (Figure S11, Supporting Information), the prediction efficiency may be lower since the extrapolative power of an ML model is almost always lower than the interpolative one. The inter-and extrapolation ability of the model is planned to be further improved by expanding the database and introducing new descriptors of nanomaterials. This platform can be further broadened on the other types of catalytic activities and nanomaterials due to regression model indifference to the type of data to be predicted as well as due to the synthesis  route-independent  nanozyme  descriptors  derived from  fundamental  element  constants  and  material  physicochemical properties descriptors.


# 3. Conclusions


In  this  work,  for  the  first  time,  an  open-access  database  of > 300metal-,  bimetal-,  metal  oxide-,  and  bimetal  oxide-based nanozymes was collected and fully curated, where scarce data was  treated  with  kNN  algorithm  with  maximal20%  scarcity

threshold and analyzed via PCA and meta-analysis to provide further  synthesis-by-design  worldwide  development  of  novel nanozymes.  Quantitative  descriptors  of  nanozyme  elemental composition-such as mX and mCD showing a strong influence on the catalytic activity and model performance expressed in  high  AFI  values  (0.50,  0.23and0.49,  0.22for Km, K cat , for mX and mCD, respectively)-and morphology were introduced  to  describe  nanozymes  and  their  catalytic  properties comprehensively.  In  particular,  lower  mX  was  shown  in  AFI analysis  to  result  in  lower Km and K cat,  whereas  mCD shows the  opposite  influence.  Thus,  these  descriptors  introduced provide  physical  insights  into  nanozyme  efficacy,  which  is very  useful  for  experimenters  working  on  novel  nanozyme discovery.

ML  model  selection  with  hyperparameters  (for  instance, number  of  estimators,  minimal  number  of  samples  per  leaf, etc.  for  RFR)  tuning  and  Bayesian  optimization  were  implemented to choose the best algorithm from6models including LR,  DT,  SVM,  GBR,  ANN,  and  RFR,  able  to  reveal  the  quantitative relationships between the nanozyme properties and its catalytic  activities,  where  RFR  has  shown  the  best  initial  performance R2 = 0.33for lgKm  and R2 = 0.52for lgK cat , where the logarithmic  scale  rationale  was  to  equalize  the  model  distinction between order of magnitude differences such as between0.01/0.1and1/10.  Correlation-based  feature  selection  via  Correlation matrix with the threshold of0.5was shown to drastically  improve  the  model  performance proving the importance of  proper  knowledge-based  ML  model  development,  further elevating  the  model  performance in comparison with the raw dataset  from0.33to0.65for  lgKm,  and  from0.52to0.8for lgKcat .

The  Final  RFR  model  inter-and  extrapolative  power  penalized  by  the | lg K | 1m/ cat ∆ = border  as  well  as  the  reasons  for abnormal  deviations  of  predicted  values  from  the  real  values (such  as  under-represented  concentration  and  activity  constant  ranges)  were  evaluated  to  provide  current  model  limitations influencing single predictions and highlighting the gaps to be filled in the future-such as under-represented Km value ranges,  deficiency  in  'bad'  nanozymes,  data  scarcity,  and  the lack  of  quantitative  structural  descriptors  for  complex  coreshell, hollow and organic-inorganic hybrid nanozymes to name a  few-to  further  improve  the  model  predictive  power  and interpretability.

The  model  ability  to  work  on  the  experimentally  obtained data  for  ongoing  nanozymes  with  unknown  activities  demonstrating mean deviations of | lg K | 0.45m ∆ = and | lg K | 0.47cat ∆ = (which is in agreement with the state-of-art model performance | lg K | 0.44m ∆ = and | lg K | 0.32cat ∆ = ),  as  well  as  to  extrapolate on the samples under-represented in the collected databasesuch  as  bimetallic  nanozymes-was  demonstrated  on  the4nanozymes synthesized, characterized, and tested in16experiments  with  varying  measurement  conditions  on  peroxidase activity expressed in both Km and K cat .  The model was able to reflect the greater Km activity of crystalline FeCo in comparison with the amorphous one (predicted Km of0.21and0.30, respectively; see samples14and10in Table S6, Supporting Information), which was observed experimentally (real Km of0.24and0.39,  respectively;  see  samples14and10in  Table  S5,  respectively, Supporting Information).

Finally,  open  access  web  resource  DiZyme  was  created  containing  a  user-expandable  database  of  nanomaterials  with  catalytic  activity,  an  interactive  visualizer,  and  ML  model  query  of3difficulty  levels  that  quantitatively  predict  the  catalytic  activity expressed as the Km and K cat  with good performance. The expandability  of  the  resource  provides  continuous  model  self-improvement based on the new samples provided by the users worldwide. The appearance of aggregated and well-curated nanozyme-related data provided in this and other articles [7] will definitely provide a rapid  data-driven  novel  nanozyme  discovery.  This  resource  will facilitate  the  synthesis-by-design  approach  implementation  and optimization  of  nanomaterials  with  the  desired  catalytic  activity and open new frontiers for nanozyme rational design by enabling rapid estimation of nanomaterial potential catalytic activity prior to  tedious,  numerous,  and  resource-intensive  experiments.  Furthermore, due to its design and structure, the platform can easily be expanded to cover more materials, more catalytic activities, and the implementation of new functionality.

# THEORETICAL PART



# Database collection and curation


The database was collected from the articles containing data about nanozyme physicochemical properties,  the  conditions  of  its  synthesis  and  analysis,  and  its  measured  enzyme-like  activity, thus any scarce data was avoided (Figure S1). Articles were searched for on the Google Scholar resource (https://scholar.google.ru) using the keywords containing:  "nanozyme",  "nanomaterial with catalytic activity", "peroxidase activity", "oxidase activity", "catalase activity", "nanomaterials with catalytic activity", "formulas of nanozymes", "nanoparticles with catalytic activity", "nanoparticles catalyst".

The catalytic activity of nanomaterials is subject to Michaelis-Menten kinetics and is standardly evaluated  by  the  following  kinetic  parameters:  Michaelis-Menten  constant  (Km,  mM)  -  the substrate  concentration  required  to  achieve  half  the  maximum  enzyme  rate,  the  catalytic  rate constant (Kcat, s -1 )  -  the  number  of  turns-number of substrate molecules that each  enzyme site converts into product per unit time, considering reaction rate (Vmax, mM/s) and the concentration of catalyst (mM).

Therefore, we calculated and selected essential parameters for predicting the catalytic activity of nanozymes, describing the composition as mean electronegativity, mean redox potential, mean charge density; material properties as crystal system; shape as dimensionality; size as volume; synthesis  conditions  as  the  presence/absence  of  a  neutral  polymer  and  surfactant;  analysis conditions as pH, temperature, substrate type, H2O2, substrate and catalyst concentrations.

The categorical parameter, which is crystal system, was encoded as follows: 1 - triclinic, 2monoclinic, 3 - orthorhombic, 4 - tetragonal, 5 - trigonal, 6 - hexagonal, 7 - cubic.

Nanozymes were categorized according  to  the  parameter  dimensionality  into  three  categories, where first (1D) are nanoparticles with one of the sizes >6times larger than the others, second (2D) category are the particles that had one of the dimensions >6times smaller than the others, the rest were assigned to3rd (3D) type, were the value choice was motivated by the avoidance of class under-representation in the collected dataset.

Processed  dataset  was  formed  in  the  process  of  feature  selection,  implemented via novel elemental descriptors introduction as well as Correlation matrix analysis presented on Fig. S3on

the initially collected data. Table S2represents the head of this resulting database, including1) activity  parameters  to  be  predicted  (lgKm  and  lgKcat),  2)  nanozyme  dimensionality,  3)  crystal system,  4)  neutral  polymer  presence,  5)  surfactant  presence,  6)  substrate  type,  7)  H2O2concentration, 8) substrate concentration, 9) nanozyme concentration, 10) pH, 11) temperature, 12) mean electronegativity (mX), 13) mean reduction-oxidation potential (mRedOx), 14) mean charge density (mCD), and15) cell volume.

Missing data values were curated using k-nearest neighbor algorithm. The idea behind k-NN methods is to identify " k" samples in a data set that are similar or close in space. We then use these " k " samples to estimate the value of missing data points. The missing values of each sample are calculated using the average of the " k " - neighbors found in the data set.

# Data analysis


To introduce standard decency rules for nanozymes rational design, data analysis was performed on the collected data. The task of data analysis was to find statistically significant differences in the peroxidase and oxidase activities, as well as Vmax of nanozymes differing in one or the other feature,  as  well  as  to  introduce  new  potential  descriptors  to  describe  nanozyme  properties, including  for  future  machine  learning.  Principal  component  analysis  (PCA)  ( Fig.  S4 )  was performed to determine the distribution of parameters and estimate their importance, a tool to determine the significant axes of variance in a data set that facilitates the examination of data to understand key variables in the data and identify outliers.  A PCA analysis of the importance of the features ( Fig. S5B )  showed that the most significant parameters are the number of metals, dimensionality, polymer use in synthesis, crystal system, temperature, and mean electronegativity. The distribution of nanozymes data is shown using box-and-whiskers charts, which represent a one-dimensional probability distribution and allow to determine the degree of scattering and asymmetry in the data. Fig. S6C-D shows that the difference in peroxidase and oxidase activity between metal oxide and bimetal oxide particles is not significant (p > 0.05). However, in the case of Km peroxidase activity, we can see differences in the boxes (p < 0.05), which  appeared  due  to  a  separate  group  of  bimetallic  oxide  nanoparticles  Mn-Fe  and  Co-Fe, which  have  high  Kcat  at  small  catalyst  concentrations  ( Fig.  S6E )  and  are  the  most  promising candidates for catalyzing the reaction. A detailed analysis of bimetallic oxide nanoparticles using the box plots ( Fig. S6G-H ) revealed a significant difference in this group in terms of both Km (p < 0.0001) and Kcat (p < 0.00001). Moreover, the surface coating so widely used for nanoparticle development was found to impair both peroxidase and oxidase activity, as shown in Fig. S6A-B . This  can  be  explained  by  the  shielding  of  the  surface-active  groups  involved  in  the  catalytic reaction, whereas the surface coating itself is usually inert. In addition, the surface coating can prevent  diffusion  of  the  substrate  to  the  nanozyme  surface,  as  well  as  adsorb  it  physically  or chemically. However, not in every case, the coating is inert. For instance, one work has shown that the surface coating works in synergy with the nanoparticle and increases the specificity of the  nanoparticle,  suggesting  specificity  at  the  cost  of  efficiency  as  a  target  characteristic  in nanomaterial design. An exponential relationship with an accuracy R2 = 0.96 ( Fig. S5F ) was also found  between  the  distribution  of  nanozyme  mean  charge  density  and  the  zeta  potential measured  at  pH  =  7.0,  making  the  mean  charge  density  parameter  a  potential  descriptor  for determining  the  zeta  potential,  an  important  parameter  for  characterizing  nanoparticles  that  is often missing. Also seen in this graph are emissions that are nanozymes, whose synthesis used neutrally charged polymers that influence the surface charge. The data analysis not only shows

the  general  distribution  and  dependencies  in  and  also  indicates  the  correlation  between  the selected and calculated descriptors and the catalytic activity of nanomaterials.

Data analysis was performed for both primary and multivariate data to understand correlations between data better. GlueViz (version1.0.0), implemented in Anaconda2020.11and OriginPro2021, was used to visualize the data.

PCA is a tool for determining the significant axes of variance in a dataset and makes it easy to examine  the  data  to  understand  the  key  variables  in  the  data  and  identify  outliers.  Jupiter programs, Anaconda2020.11, performed a PCA analysis ( Fig. S4 , S5 ) of the collected database to determine the distribution of parameters and assess their importance.

# EXPERIMENTAL PART



# Materials


Iron  (II)  chloride  tetrahydrate  (99.9%),  Iron  (III)  chloride  hexahydrate  (99.9%),  zinc  chloride tetrahydrate (99.9%), cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate (99.9%), pyrrole (99.9%), sodium borohydride (99.9%), 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (99.9%), sodium hydroxide (99.9%) were  purchased  from  Sigma-Aldrich.  Sodium  acetate  trihydrate  (99.9%)  was  purchased  from Dia-m  (Russia).  Hydrogen  peroxide  (35%),  acetic  acid  (99.8%)  were  purchased  from  Vekton (Saint-Petersburg,  Russia).  Dimethyl  sulfoxide  (99.5%)  was  purchased  from  Ekos  (Russia). Aqueous ammonia solution (25%) was purchased from Sigma Tec (Russia).


# Synthesis


1) Fe3O4

The Fe3O4nanoparticles were made using the coprecipitation process, as described previously. 109 2.5g FeCl2·4H2O and5g of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in100ml of deionized water. After that, 11mL of aqueous ammonia solution was added to the solution and mixed for1minute at room temperature with continual stirring (500rpm). A magnet was used to capture the magnetite nanoparticles, which were then cleaned in deionized water until they reached a pH of neutral. Particle deposition using a magnetic  field, decantation, water addition,  and  mechanical resuspension  of  the  sediment  were  all  part  of  the  magnetic  separation  process.  The  resulting black  precipitate  was  mixed  with100mL  of  deionized  water  and  subjected  to  ultrasonic treatment  (30kHz,  110W)  under  constant  stirring  for2h.  Finally,  the  solution  of  magnetite nanoparticles was washed and filtered three times with a magnet.


# 2) ZnFe2O4


0.87g ZnCl2·4H2O and2.24g FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in25ml of deionized water for30minutes with magnetic stirring. Then, with magnetic stirring, a2M NaOH solution was gradually added to the chloride solution to get the pH up to11. The resulting mixture was placed in a95mL Teflon autoclave (fill factor = 0.8) and heated at150 °C for10hours at a rate of5degrees per minute in an oven. The contents of the autoclave were rinsed and filtered five times with a magnet after cooling  to  ambient  temperature  naturally.  The  residual  was  dried  in  oven  for12hours at95 ℃.

3) FeCo (amorphous)

The amorphous particles were prepared as follows. 0.135g FeCl2·4H2O, 0.173g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and0.750mL pyrrole were dissolved in20mL distilled water. Likewise, 0.650g  NaBH4was  dissolved  in10mL  distilled  water  and  added  dropwise  into  the  solution  of precursor  salts  during  continual  stirring  (300rpm).  The  reaction  would  take20minutes  to complete before the precipitate appeared. Then, the precipitate was washed several times with distilled water and gathered by an external magnet. Finally, the particles were freeze-dried for6hours.


# 4) FeCo (crystalline)


To obtain crystalline FeCo, the synthesized amorphous FeCo sample was annealed in an Ar gas stream at550°C for10minutes.


# Material characterization


All  the  materials  synthesized  were  characterized  using  scanning  electron  microscopy  (SEM, Tescan  VEGA3SBH)  for  relatively  large  nanozymes  and  transmission  electron  microscopy (TEM,  Jeol  JEM2100)  for  nanozymes  smaller  than100nm,  X-ray  diffractometer  (Rigaku SmartLab3), dynamic light scattering (Photocor Compact-Z), where the sample preparation was basically the preparation of nanozyme powders and preparation of diluted solutions.


# Peroxidase activity assessment


The  peroxidase  activity  study  was  performed  based  on  a  standardized  protocol108with modifications.  Briefly, prepare solution of nanozyme with a concentration5 µg in1mL of HAcNaAc buffers with pH3.6and4. Various volumes (1to10 μl) of the initial TMB solution (10mg mL -1in DMSO) were added to the250 µl of nanozyme solution in different cells. 10 µl of30% hydrogen  peroxide  was  added.  Mix  the  samples,  place  them  in  a30  °C  and37  °C  and record the initial rate of change of absorbance at652nm on UV/VIS spectrometer (the Spark, Tecan). Convert the rate of increase in absorption to the concentration of the substrate expressed in micromoles  per  minute  (i.e.,  the rate of catalytic reaction, ν), and  plot against the concentration of the substrate to obtain the Michaelis-Menten curve.  From the resulting graph, calculate Km and the maximum rate and then Kcat.

# Web resource DiZyme


Web  resource  structure  (Figure  S14):  open  database  of  existing  inorganic  nanozymes  with original article links, data visualization tool, multi-level prediction of peroxidase activity, based on the request, data loading, if you want to add new nanozyme to the platform.

The resource offers a predictive algorithm for3different levels of user query: The basic level involves an initial step with the catalytic activity of the material, the user enters only the material formula,  and  the  output  is  a  table  with  different  ranges  of  Km  and  Kcat,  under  standard peroxidase-like activity assay108for  different  nanozyme sizes. The progressive level assumes complete knowledge of the physical characteristics of the nanomaterial, on which the result is a variation  of  the  target  constants  depending  on  each  of  the  analysis  conditions.  The  advanced level,  on  the  other  hand,  is  needed  for  researchers  who  use  their  methodology  to  analyze  the peroxidase  activity  of  the  target  nanomaterial,  so  the  user  needs  to  enter  all13parameters (formula,  crystal  system,  length,  width,  depth,  neutral  polymer,  surfactant,  substrate  type,  pH, temperature, substrate, H2O2and catalyst concentrations) to predict Km and Kcat.

The open web resource was made in Python (version3.9) using the Django framework (version3.2) in the PyCharm Professional2021.1and implemented on the server. The nanozyme data is stored in a relational database made in Django, which also has a built-in machine learning model that predicts the enzyme-like activity of the nanozyme on request based on the data entered. The front-end development was created using the Django-Bootstrap framework (version15.0.0) and HTML language.
